meta.purpose: Provide concrete examples of good and bad architecture reasoning judgment.
meta.load_condition: Load when examples or cases are needed to illustrate architecture reasoning judgment.

cases.title: Deep module design enables independent evolution
cases.context: A PaymentService has 2 public methods: `charge(amount, customerId)` and `refund(transactionId)`. Internally, it handles 5 payment providers, retry logic, idempotency, and fraud detection.
cases.what_happened: When the team needed to add a new payment provider, only PaymentService's internals changed. No caller of `charge` or `refund` was affected. The deep interface (2 methods) hid the complexity (5 providers + retry + fraud), allowing the internals to evolve without breaking callers.
cases.what_was_learned: Deep modules create architectural stability. When the interface is small and stable, the implementation can change freely. When the interface is large and volatile, every internal change risks breaking callers.
cases.structural_pattern: Simple interface → Hidden complexity → Internal evolution without caller impact

cases.title: Shallow module creates cascading changes
cases.context: A UserPreferences module exposes 15 public getters and setters for individual preference fields: `getEmailNotifications()`, `setEmailNotifications()`, `getTheme()`, `setTheme()`, etc.
cases.what_happened: When the team needed to add preference validation (theme must be one of 3 values, email frequency must match the subscription tier), they had to modify every caller that used `setTheme()` and `setEmailFrequency()`. Because the module was shallow (exposing individual fields), validation could not be hidden inside it. 12 files across 4 modules needed changes for what should have been a 1-file update.
cases.what_was_learned: Shallow modules propagate internal changes to callers. When the interface exposes implementation details (individual fields), any change to those details (adding validation) forces callers to change. A deeper interface (`updatePreferences(changes)` with internal validation) would have isolated the change.
cases.structural_pattern: Complex interface → Exposed details → Internal change forces caller changes → Cascading modification

cases.title: Zooming out reveals wrong boundaries
cases.context: A team asks the agent to add a report generation feature. The agent starts implementing it in the ReportService but discovers it needs data from UserService, OrderService, and InventoryService.
cases.what_happened: Instead of creating cross-service dependencies, the agent zoomed out and realized that the report generation feature is a cross-cutting concern that should have its own data aggregation layer. The current architecture assumed each service would be queried independently, but reports needed joined data. The agent proposed a ReportDataAggregator that subscribes to events from all three services, maintaining a denormalized view for fast report generation. This kept the existing services independent while providing the report feature with the data it needed.
cases.what_was_learned: Zooming out revealed that the feature didn't belong in any existing service — it was a new architectural component. Without zooming out, the agent would have created coupling between services, starting the decay toward a ball of mud.
cases.structural_pattern: Feature crosses boundaries → Zoom out → Identify new architectural component → Preserve existing boundaries
